chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 230
Course Title: Informatics for Industrial Engineers
Section: 500
Time: TR 02:20 - 03:35 p.m.
Location: ETB 1027
Credit Hours: Credits 3. 3 Lecture Hours.

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Yen-Jen Wang
Office: ETB 4035
Phone: 979 862-1343
E-Mail: yjhwang@tamu.edu (include “ISEN 230 Section 500” in your email subject line.)
Office Hours: Wed 1:30- 2:30 p.m. and by appointment

Office Hours: If you would like to set up a meeting, please email me 24 hours before the meeting time and suggest a few times that might work with your schedule. I will try to find a time that works for both you and the teaching team. You should prepare questions before we meet.

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Structured programming concepts for implementing mathematical and statistical models in industrial engineering problems; emphasis on introductory production and service system problems and computer-based approaches to solve the problems; engineering applications of probability and statistics concepts; includes using Excel and VBA in coding and evaluating models.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: CSCE 206, CSCE 111, CSCE 120, CSCE 121, CSCE 110 or equivalent. Co-requisites: STAT 211.

chunks.title: Course Relationship to ABET Outcomes
chunks.content: 7. an ability to acquire and apply new knowledge as needed, using appropriate learning strategies

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Use Excel and VBA within a production system context,
• Understand the basic terminology associated with production and service systems, and
• Develop simple quantitative decision models relevant to production and service systems

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content: VBA for Modelers (5th Edition) by S. Christian Albright, 2016.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Homework assignments are due before midnight on the due date. Late submission will not be accepted. Not all homework will be graded, but solutions will be posted for self-check. I strongly suggest that you do the homework (that is the only way to learn the material well.)
There will be in-class assignments / quizzes on the material (lectures, readings, and homework) covered before.
In addition, there will be two midterms and one final examination during the semester. No make-up examinations will be given unless prior arrangements have been made with the instructor.

The components of the course grade and their weights are: In-Class Assignments/Homework/Quizzes, 20%; Midterm Examination I, 25%; Midterm Examination II, 25%; and Final Examination, 30%.

The university letter grade policy is followed (>=90%: A; 80-89%: B; 70 – 79%: C; 60 – 69%: D; below 60%: F.)

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: • Late submission will not be accepted.

chunks.title: Other Course Information Items
chunks.content:
The course website will be maintained through http://canvas.tamu.edu/. This site will contain announcements and other information concerning the course. In addition, it will be used to distribute class materials (homework assignment, notes,) and grades. Check the website regularly; and information posted on it will be as valid as if it was mentioned in class.

It is expected that all work submitted in this class will be done individually. If you got any help with a particular problem from someone, then cite that person in your write-up. For example, "I thank John Smith for helping me solve this problem." If you worked together with one or more other people, then each of you should cite the other(s).

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statements
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. Artificial Intelligence (AI) text generators and natural language processing tools (colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video, and image generators should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to, Creating or revising drafts, editing your work, and reviewing a peer's work

This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable
